rationale: A is more tightly centered on scholarly contribution positioning: its early audit covers territory, bounded opportunity, a research-question candidate, novelty limits, utility, and repair, and it explicitly warns that a joint rule change does not establish complementarity or direction. It is also somewhat leaner and better sequenced. B more accurately treats the generic organization as the supplied actor, offers a particularly usable contribution paragraph, and distinguishes model-result, unsupported, not-assessed, and not-applicable statuses more cleanly. B slightly overstates the delay semantics by saying delayed information must be unavailable at decision time, while A is overly conservative in calling the actor unknown; neither rises to a material defect. Both preserve the allocation decision, capacity constraint, information delay, model-versus-empirical boundary, novelty restraint, and extension safeguards. Both also restate the same missing objective, evidence, and literature limitations across several tables and closing sections. Their opposing strengths are balanced, so the overall result is a tie.
